steps.title: Recall the finite geometric series identity
steps.content: For any value of $$p$$, the product $$(1 - p)(1 + p + p^2 + \cdots + p^n)$$ telescopes to $$1 - p^{n+1}$$. Here the second factor has terms from $$p^0$$ through $$p^6$$, so $$n = 6$$ and the product equals $$1 - p^7$$.

steps.title: Verify by direct expansion
steps.content:
Multiply $$(1-p)$$ term-by-term into the series:
  • $$1 \cdot (1 + p + p^2 + p^3 + p^4 + p^5 + p^6) = 1 + p + p^2 + p^3 + p^4 + p^5 + p^6$$
  • $$-p \cdot (1 + p + p^2 + p^3 + p^4 + p^5 + p^6) = -p - p^2 - p^3 - p^4 - p^5 - p^6 - p^7$$
Adding, every middle term cancels, leaving $$1 - p^7$$.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices
steps.content:
• Choice A: $$1 - p^8$$ — would require a factor up to $$p^7$$, not $$p^6$$. Eliminated.
  • Choice B: $$1 - p^7$$ — matches the telescoped result. This is correct.
  • Choice C: $$1 - p^6$$ — would require the series to end at $$p^5$$. Eliminated.
  • Choice D: $$1 - p^5$$ — would require the series to end at $$p^4$$. Eliminated.
That matches choice B — B is correct.
